description: Monolithic Power Systems is a fabless power semiconductor company that designs high-performance power management ICs, DC/DC converters, power modules, and related analog products for computing, automotive, communications, industrial, and consumer electronics markets.

overview.title: Business Focus
paragraphs: Monolithic Power Systems, commonly branded as MPS, builds semiconductor-based power electronics that regulate, convert, monitor, and manage power inside electronic systems. Its core pitch is compact, efficient power delivery: less board space, less heat, and fewer opportunities for engineers to spend their week negotiating with inductors.

The company’s product base spans switching converters and controllers, power modules, PMICs, battery-management products, motor drivers, sensors, isolation, AC/DC products, and inductors. The seed products already present in the registry, DC/DC converters and power modules, sit squarely in the company’s most visible product categories.

overview.title: Markets Served
paragraphs: MPS sells into several end markets, including storage and computing, enterprise data, automotive, communications, consumer, and industrial applications. Its exposure to server, cloud, AI infrastructure, automotive electronics, and telecom power systems makes it a supplier to the less glamorous but essential layer beneath modern compute demand.

The company emphasizes analog and mixed-signal power design, where products often have long life cycles and must meet tight performance, efficiency, thermal, and reliability requirements. This is not headline-grabbing silicon, but it is the sort of silicon that tends to be missed only when it fails.

overview.title: Strategic Position
paragraphs: MPS presents itself as a fabless global semiconductor company using proprietary process technology, system-level design knowledge, and application engineering to produce compact power solutions. Its origin story centers on integrating more of a power system into a single chip or module, reducing the number of external components customers need to design around.

For Free The World mapping, MPS is best treated as an enabling semiconductor supplier rather than a direct consumer platform gatekeeper. The open-alternative question is therefore less about replacing a single app or service and more about where open hardware, repairable electronics, transparent supply chains, and standards-based power designs can reduce dependence on opaque component ecosystems.
